text: Nowadays, sleep resection is widely practiced, accepted and considered standard therapy for the surgical treatment of lung cancer.
Frontal sleep lobectomy was first introduced by priest Thomas in 1947 as a means of parental mal-sparing surgery to preserve lung function.
Several surgeons promoted sleep lobectomy as an alternative to pneumonectomy for patients with non-small cell lung cancer.
Recent reports suggested that sleep lobectomy even improved the quality of life and long-term survival when compared with pneumonectomy.
Right upper sleep lobectomy is the most frequently around 70% performed bronchioplasty procedures and right pneumonectomy is the most high-risk procedure.

Although video-assisted thoracic surgery is regarded as a minimally invasive procedure, many surgeons think that BATS is too complex and has too many technical limitations to be applied to bronchioplasty.
Therefore, there are few reports in the literature of BATS bronchioplasty.
The procedure is complex, but it can safely be performed using some additional techniques.
In this video, we describe a simplified butch bronchioplasty technique performed by two porch-butt approach with no grid spreading.
